description: Cross-domain dependency edges for Domains IV-VI and connections to Domains I-III

edges.description: Vectors generalize real numbers to ordered tuples in R^n; scalars are 1-dimensional vectors
The dot product is the standard inner product in R^n, specializing the abstract inner product concept
The Euclidean norm ||v|| = sqrt(v1^2 + ... + vn^2) is the Pythagorean theorem generalized to n dimensions
Structure's Cauchy-Schwarz for general inner product spaces generalizes the scalar inequality from Perception
Eigenvalues are roots of the characteristic polynomial and may be complex even for real matrices
A linear transformation is a function between vector spaces that preserves addition and scalar multiplication
The gradient extends the single-variable derivative to a vector of partial derivatives in R^n
Divergence measures the rate of expansion of a vector field, extending the derivative to vector-valued functions
Curl measures rotational tendency of a vector field, extending the derivative to capture circulation
Stokes' theorem generalizes the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to integration over manifolds with boundary
The Divergence Theorem relates volume integrals of divergence to surface flux, generalizing FTC to 3D
Green's theorem relates a line integral around a closed curve to a double integral over the enclosed region
The Jacobian matrix generalizes the chain rule to multivariate functions; Df(x) is the linear map best approximating f near x
Complex exponentials e^(ikx) encode wave solutions; complex analysis provides the natural language for wave phenomena
Complex function theory builds on Euler's formula e^(ix) = cos(x) + i*sin(x) as the bridge between exponential and trigonometric
Contour integration generalizes definite integration from the real line to paths in the complex plane
Line integrals generalize definite integrals from intervals to curves in R^n
Least squares minimizes ||Ax - b||^2, applying optimization to find the best approximate solution
The multivariable chain rule extends the single-variable chain rule to compositions of multivariate functions
Vector space axioms require a scalar field; R (the real numbers) is the most common scalar field for real vector spaces
The time-independent Schrodinger equation H*psi = E*psi is an eigenvalue ODE/PDE for the Hamiltonian operator
The time-dependent Schrodinger equation i*hbar*dpsi/dt = H*psi is a first-order PDE in time
A Hilbert space is a complete inner product space, generalizing finite-dimensional vector spaces to infinite dimensions
Quantum operators are self-adjoint linear operators on Hilbert space; linearity is their defining structural property
The quantum wave function satisfies wave-like equations; its probability interpretation connects wave physics to measurement
The quantum harmonic oscillator is the quantized version of the classical oscillator, with discrete energy levels
Heisenberg uncertainty mirrors the time-frequency uncertainty: narrower wave packets require broader frequency spectra
Dimensional analysis uses rational exponents to express how physical quantities scale; dimensions are rational power products
Physical constants are real-valued measurements; c = 299,792,458 m/s requires the real number system
Quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s) are integers or half-integers; discreteness is their defining feature
Electron configuration is determined by filling orbitals according to quantum number constraints
Expectation values <A> = integral of psi* A psi dx require definite integration over all space
Dirac ket notation |psi> represents quantum states as vectors in Hilbert space, applying vector formalism to physics
The commutator [A,B] = AB - BA uses matrix/operator multiplication; non-commutativity is key to quantum mechanics
Quantum superposition expresses states as linear combinations of basis states, applying linear independence and span
de Broglie wavelength lambda = h/p assigns a wavelength to massive particles, applying wave concepts to matter
Planck's blackbody radiation law relates spectral radiance to frequency, applying wave frequency concepts to thermal radiation
E = mc^2 requires the speed of light as the conversion factor between mass and energy
Groups formalize the algebraic structure of invertible linear transformations; GL(n) is the general linear group
The field axioms generalize the algebraic properties of real numbers to abstract systems with +, *, and inverses
The complex numbers form a field; field theory generalizes their algebraic structure
Every normed vector space induces a topology via the norm metric; topology generalizes the notion of 'nearness' beyond linear structure
Topological continuity (preimage of open is open) generalizes epsilon-delta continuity from calculus
Natural numbers can be constructed from sets (von Neumann ordinals); set theory provides the foundational language for number systems
Formal set theory (ZFC axioms) generalizes the informal set notation used throughout Perception
Equality of real numbers is an equivalence relation; equivalence relations formalize the concept of 'sameness' used throughout mathematics
The integers form a ring; ring theory generalizes integer arithmetic (addition, multiplication, but not always division)
Homeomorphism is the topological version of isomorphism: a bicontinuous bijection preserving topological structure
Compactness extends the notion of closed and bounded sets; continuous images of compact sets are compact (extreme value theorem)
Propositional logic formalizes the logical connectives (and, or, not) used informally in set membership and conditions
Mathematical induction is defined on the natural numbers and requires their well-ordering property
Metric spaces generalize the Euclidean distance formula to abstract distance functions satisfying the triangle inequality
The fundamental group pi_1(X,x_0) is a group; its construction requires group theory (composition of loops)
Symmetry groups often consist of orthogonal transformations (rotations, reflections) represented as orthogonal matrices
Galois theory studies field extensions and their automorphism groups; it requires the full machinery of field theory
Complex analysis relies on topological concepts (open sets, connectedness, compactness) for its fundamental theorems
The Hilbert space inner product generalizes the dot product to infinite-dimensional and complex spaces
The determinant is a group homomorphism from GL(n) to the multiplicative group of the scalar field
Linear transformations are homomorphisms of vector spaces; homomorphism theory generalizes this to arbitrary algebraic structures
Born's rule assigns probabilities to measurement outcomes that form a discrete or continuous set; set theory frames the sample space
Observable eigenvalues are the possible measurement outcomes; the spectral theorem guarantees real eigenvalues for self-adjoint operators
Topological continuity provides the rigorous foundation for holomorphic functions, which are continuous maps in the complex topology
Spin is described by representations of SU(2), a Lie group; group theory classifies spin states and their transformations
Formal axiomatic systems motivate rigorous proofs of calculus theorems like MVT, replacing intuitive arguments with logical deduction
The spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators motivates the mathematical framework of quantum measurement
SVD provides optimal low-rank approximations and solves least-squares problems, applying optimization to matrix factorization
Predicate logic extends propositional logic with quantifiers (forall, exists) and variables, enabling statements about mathematical objects

compositionRules.yields: Jacobian matrix of a multivariable function
compositionRules.example: The Jacobian of f(x,y) = (x^2+y, xy) is the matrix of partial derivatives [[2x,1],[y,x]]

compositionRules.yields: Representation theory: groups acting as linear transformations on vector spaces
conditions: Group is finite or Lie group
Vector space is over characteristic 0 field
compositionRules.example: The rotation group SO(3) represented by 3x3 orthogonal matrices acting on R^3

compositionRules.yields: Quantum measurement framework: eigenvalues as outcomes, eigenstates as post-measurement states
compositionRules.example: Measuring energy via Hamiltonian H|n> = E_n|n>; outcome E_n with probability |<n|psi>|^2

compositionRules.yields: Topological group: a group with continuous group operations
conditions: Multiplication map G x G -> G is continuous
Inversion map G -> G is continuous
compositionRules.example: GL(n,R) with the subspace topology from R^(n^2) is a topological group

compositionRules.yields: Riemann surfaces: complex manifolds where multi-valued functions become single-valued
compositionRules.example: The Riemann surface of sqrt(z) is a double cover of C branched at the origin

compositionRules.yields: Formal proof system: axioms + inference rules producing verified theorems
compositionRules.example: ZFC set theory + first-order logic produces all of standard mathematics

compositionRules.yields: Energy levels: discrete eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian operator
compositionRules.example: Hydrogen atom: E_n = -13.6/n^2 eV are eigenvalues of the Coulomb Hamiltonian

compositionRules.yields: Spectral theory: decomposition of operators via complete orthonormal bases
compositionRules.example: Fourier basis {e^(inx)} is a complete orthonormal set in L^2([0,2pi]); spectral decomposition uses similar bases

compositionRules.yields: Algebraic topology: using group-theoretic invariants to classify topological spaces
conditions: Groups are computed via homotopy or homology
compositionRules.example: pi_1(S^1) = Z distinguishes the circle from the disk; pi_1(disk) = 0

compositionRules.yields: Natural units: c = hbar = 1 simplifies equations by eliminating dimensional constants
conditions: All quantities expressed in powers of energy
compositionRules.example: In natural units, E^2 = p^2 + m^2 instead of E^2 = (pc)^2 + (mc^2)^2

compositionRules.yields: Topological vector space: a vector space with a topology making operations continuous
conditions: Addition V x V -> V is continuous
Scalar multiplication F x V -> V is continuous
compositionRules.example: Every normed space is a topological vector space; L^p spaces are Banach (complete normed) spaces

compositionRules.yields: Symmetry classification of particles: irreducible representations label particle types
conditions: Symmetry group acts on quantum states
compositionRules.example: SU(3) flavor symmetry classifies quarks into multiplets (up, down, strange in the fundamental representation)

compositionRules.yields: Polynomial ring factorization: formal power series as elements of the ring R[[x]]
conditions: Series converges or is treated formally
compositionRules.example: Z[x]/(x^2+1) is isomorphic to the Gaussian integers Z[i]; Taylor series live in R[[x]]

compositionRules.yields: Gravitational force field: F = -grad(phi) where phi = -GM/r is the gravitational potential
compositionRules.example: Earth's gravitational field g = -grad(-GM/r) points radially inward with magnitude GM/r^2

compositionRules.yields: Quotient group: G/N where the equivalence classes are cosets of a normal subgroup N
conditions: N is a normal subgroup of G
compositionRules.example: Z/nZ: integers modulo n form a cyclic group of order n

compositionRules.yields: Matrix mechanics: quantum mechanics formulated as matrix operations on state vectors
conditions: Basis is chosen for Hilbert space
Operators represented as matrices in that basis
compositionRules.example: Pauli matrices sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z represent spin-1/2 operators in the |up>, |down> basis

compositionRules.yields: Physical scattering amplitudes computed via residues at poles in the complex energy plane
conditions: Contour can be closed in the complex plane
compositionRules.example: Evaluating integral_(-inf)^(inf) dx/(x^2+1) = pi via a semicircular contour and the residue at z=i

compositionRules.yields: Uniform convergence: sequences of functions converging uniformly preserve continuity
conditions: Function space has the sup metric
Convergence is uniform, not just pointwise
compositionRules.example: If f_n -> f uniformly and each f_n is continuous, then f is continuous (uniform limit theorem)

compositionRules.yields: Maxwell's equations in integral form: relating field circulation and flux to sources
conditions: Fields are smooth vector fields in R^3
Surfaces and curves are appropriately oriented
compositionRules.example: Faraday's law: integral of E dot dl = -d/dt integral of B dot dA (curl E relates to changing B flux)

compositionRules.yields: Dimension theory: topological invariants capturing the 'size' of a space (covering dimension, Hausdorff dimension)
compositionRules.example: R^n has covering dimension n; the Cantor set has topological dimension 0 but Hausdorff dimension log(2)/log(3)

compositionRules.yields: Characteristic equation of observables: det(A - lambda I) = 0 determines the measurement spectrum
conditions: Operator is represented as a finite matrix
compositionRules.example: For spin-1/2 sigma_z = [[1,0],[0,-1]], det(sigma_z - lambda I) = 0 gives lambda = +/-1

compositionRules.yields: Cayley's theorem verification: every finite group of order n embeds in S_n, proved by induction on group structure
compositionRules.example: Z/3Z embeds in S_3 via the permutation representation: 0->(1), 1->(123), 2->(132)

compositionRules.yields: Solution of linear ODE systems via diagonalization: x'=Ax solved by x(t) = P*diag(e^(lambda_i*t))*P^(-1)*x(0)
compositionRules.example: x'=[[0,1],[-2,-3]]x: eigenvalues -1,-2; solution x(t) = c1*e^(-t)*v1 + c2*e^(-2t)*v2

compositionRules.yields: Bandwidth theorem: the product of time duration and frequency bandwidth is bounded below
conditions: Time and frequency representations are Fourier duals
compositionRules.example: A Gaussian pulse sigma_t * sigma_f >= 1/(4*pi) achieves the minimum uncertainty product
